directive: Implement cutover as a feature-flag per-rule mechanism, inline in the existing alerting service. A per-rule flag (e.g., a config or DB field) determines whether that rule's alert routes to the old dispatcher or PagerDuty — never both simultaneously. Roll forward in batches: flip rules to PD, validate, continue. Rollback for any rule: flip its flag back to the old dispatcher. Expose the current routing state of each rule (old vs PD) in the observability dashboard. Do not implement a comparator service or any dual-write period.
rationale: Option A is disqualified by two independent constraints: (1) a comparator service is a separate new service, violating the project non-goal 'do not deploy a separate new service'; (2) dual-write routes real alerts through both systems simultaneously, directly contradicting the p6 acceptance criterion 'zero double-dispatch.' Between B and C, Option B (feature-flag per-rule) is the correct fit. Each rule routes to exactly one system at any given moment, satisfying zero-double-dispatch. Rollback is a flag flip — trivially within 5 minutes. No maintenance window is required, no new deployment artifact, and flag state is naturally exposed to the observability dashboard. Option C (blue-green) requires scheduling a maintenance window and pre-rehearsing a rollback script — a heavier operational lift than the non-goal ('dispatcher lives inline in the existing alerting service') and the acceptance criteria require. The phase spec is ambiguous on mechanism only; the acceptance criteria are sufficient as written. No plan amendment needed.
